content:
First, set up coordinates.  Let the big circle \(C_2\) have center \(O=(0,0)\) and radius \(9\).  The small circle \(C_1\) has radius \(r\) and is tangent internally at \(A=(9,0)\), so its center is 
\[
C=(9-r,0)=:(d,0)\,,
\]
with \(d=9-r\).

We look for a point \(P=(x,y)\) on \(C_1\) such that the tangent to \(C_1\) at \(P\) passes through \(O\).  Equivalently, \(\overrightarrow{OP}\perp\overrightarrow{CP}\).  In coordinates this gives
\[
|P-C|^2=r^2,\quad P\cdot(P-C)=0.
\]
One finds
\[
x=\frac{d^2-r^2}{d},\quad 
y^2=d\,x-\!x^2,
\]
and hence
\[
|P|^2=x^2+y^2=d\,x=\;d^2-r^2,\quad |P|=\sqrt{d^2-r^2}\,. 
\]

The line \(OP\) meets the big circle again at 
\[
Q\ =\ \frac{9}{|P|}\,P,
\]
and meets the vertical tangent at \(A\) (the line \(x=9\)) at
\[
R\ =\ \frac{9}{x}\,P.
\]
Since \(P,Q,R\) lie on the same ray and we are given \(PQ=QR\), one finds the parameter‐condition
\[
\frac{9}{|P|}-1 \;=\;\frac{9}{x}-\frac{9}{|P|}\,,
\]
which after using \(|P|^2=d^2-r^2\) and \(d=9-r\) forces \(d=5\), \(r=4\).  Then
\[
|P|=\sqrt{d^2-r^2}=\sqrt{25-16}=3,
\]
so 
\[
\boxed{3}
\]
is the required length of \(OP\).

message.content:
First, set up coordinates.  Let the big circle \(C_2\) have center \(O=(0,0)\) and radius \(9\).  The small circle \(C_1\) has radius \(r\) and is tangent internally at \(A=(9,0)\), so its center is 
\[
C=(9-r,0)=:(d,0)\,,
\]
with \(d=9-r\).

We look for a point \(P=(x,y)\) on \(C_1\) such that the tangent to \(C_1\) at \(P\) passes through \(O\).  Equivalently, \(\overrightarrow{OP}\perp\overrightarrow{CP}\).  In coordinates this gives
\[
|P-C|^2=r^2,\quad P\cdot(P-C)=0.
\]
One finds
\[
x=\frac{d^2-r^2}{d},\quad 
y^2=d\,x-\!x^2,
\]
and hence
\[
|P|^2=x^2+y^2=d\,x=\;d^2-r^2,\quad |P|=\sqrt{d^2-r^2}\,. 
\]

The line \(OP\) meets the big circle again at 
\[
Q\ =\ \frac{9}{|P|}\,P,

\[
R\ =\ \frac{9}{x}\,P.
\]

\[
\frac{9}{|P|}-1 \;=\;\frac{9}{x}-\frac{9}{|P|}\,,

\[

\[
\boxed{3}
\]
is the required length of \(OP\).
